conclusion: volume-gated trend bias did not improve viability relative to ungated c1 under the same 5-cohort contract (6/9). Same failing gates: absolute return, IR, cost_stress.

candidate.gate_definition: raw amount > rolling 20d median amount (excl current day), strict >

failure_analysis.cost_stress_not_improved: Volume gate did not improve cost_stress because the current cost-stress model uses fixed slippage (20bp open + 20bp close) rather than liquidity-sensitive slippage. Excluding low-volume stocks does not reduce assumed trading costs under this model.
failure_analysis.diversification_loss: Gate excluded 52.72% of stock-days. Reduced candidate pool led to lower topk perturbation robustness (0.106 to 0.062) and higher cost sensitivity.
failure_analysis.alpha_slight_improvement: Return improved marginally (+0.023) but not enough to offset IR and cost_stress deterioration. Gate removed some noise but not enough to change the viability tier.
failure_analysis.root_cause: Fixed-slippage cost model renders volume-based conditioning ineffective. A conditioning strategy that targets the actual cost model (e.g., turnover compression, weight capping) would be more aligned with the cost_stress gate.

strategic_closure: intraday microstructure standalone line is now closed across three attempts: 26-cohort baseline (5/9), 5-cohort contract redesign (6/9), and volume-gated trend-bias conditioning (6/9).
next_direction_candidate: Investigate whether a fundamentally different signal family (e.g., overnight-gap-based or cross-asset momentum) can provide positive absolute return under the existing 5-cohort contract.
